title: Module 11: The Pipe Obstacle Blueprint
desc: Build the pipe obstacle actor with top and bottom meshes, scrolling movement, and a gap the bird must fly through.

steps.title: The Pipe Actor Structure
steps.body: In the **Content Browser**, right-click and create a new **Blueprint Class** using `Actor` as the parent. Name it `BP_Pipe`. Open it. We need three components: a top pipe mesh, a bottom pipe mesh, and a gap trigger box. Click **Add Component** and add a **Static Mesh** named `TopPipe`. Add another **Static Mesh** named `BottomPipe`. Finally, add a **Box Collision** named `ScoreTrigger`. The `ScoreTrigger` box will sit in the gap between the two pipes — when the bird passes through it, we increment the score. Make a `Scene Component` the root and attach all three to it. This clean hierarchy keeps the pipe as one self-contained actor.
steps.tip: Use a plain `Scene Component` as the root rather than a mesh — it gives you a clean transform origin without visual baggage.

steps.q: Why is a `Box Collision` component placed in the gap between the two pipe meshes?
opts: To prevent the bird from passing through the gap
To detect when the bird successfully passes through the gap so the score can be incremented
To add physics simulation to the pipe gap
To make the gap visible to the player
steps.explain: The `Box Collision` in the gap acts as a score trigger — it detects overlap events. When the bird enters this invisible box, we know it successfully navigated the gap and we award a point. It does NOT block movement; its collision profile is set to overlap-only.

steps.title: Assigning Pipe Meshes
steps.body: Select `TopPipe` in the **Components** panel. In the **Details** panel under **Static Mesh**, assign a tall, thin cube mesh. If you enabled Starter Content, use the `Cube` from `Engine Content`. Scale it to `X=1, Y=1, Z=5` to make it tall. Position its `Relative Location` to `Z=400` (above the gap). Select `BottomPipe` and use the same mesh with the same scale. Position it at `Z=-400` (below the gap). The gap between them is now 800 units wide by default. Select `ScoreTrigger` and set its `Box Extent` to `X=50, Y=100, Z=350` — large enough to catch the bird reliably. Set its `Relative Location` to `Z=0` so it sits exactly in the center of the gap.
steps.tip: Keep the gap size between 250-400 units for a fair but challenging experience — playtesting will tell you the sweet spot.

steps.q: The gap between TopPipe (Z=400) and BottomPipe (Z=-400) is 800 units. If the bird's collision sphere has a radius of 32 units, what is the effective gap the player must navigate?
opts: 800 units
736 units — the gap minus the bird's diameter on each side
400 units
832 units
steps.explain: The effective gap is 800 minus the bird's full diameter (32 × 2 = 64), giving 736 units of navigable space. This is why tuning the gap with a real collision sphere in mind matters — the visual gap always appears larger than what the player can actually use.

steps.title: Configuring Collision Profiles
steps.body: Select `TopPipe` in the **Components** panel. In **Details**, find the **Collision** section. Set **Collision Presets** to `BlockAllDynamic`. Do the same for `BottomPipe`. This means the bird will physically collide with the pipes and trigger an overlap event. For `ScoreTrigger`, set **Collision Presets** to `OverlapAllDynamic`. This means the box does NOT block anything — it only fires overlap events. The critical distinction is: `Block` stops movement and triggers Hit events, while `Overlap` allows pass-through and triggers Overlap events. Mixing these up is the most common beginner collision mistake in UE5.
steps.tip: When in doubt, open the **Collision Presets** dropdown and read the tooltip — each preset clearly states what it blocks and what it overlaps.

steps.q: What is the key difference between setting a collision component to `BlockAllDynamic` versus `OverlapAllDynamic`?
opts: BlockAllDynamic makes the object invisible; OverlapAllDynamic makes it visible
BlockAllDynamic physically stops objects and fires Hit events; OverlapAllDynamic allows pass-through and fires Overlap events without stopping movement
They are identical — both stop movement and fire events
OverlapAllDynamic is only used for triggers; BlockAllDynamic is only for walls
steps.explain: This distinction is fundamental. `BlockAllDynamic` creates a physical barrier — the bird bounces off or stops, triggering `OnComponentHit`. `OverlapAllDynamic` is a ghost volume — objects pass through it freely, triggering `OnComponentBeginOverlap`. Using the wrong one means either your score trigger blocks the bird, or your pipe walls let the bird fly through them.

steps.title: Pipe Scrolling Movement
steps.body: In `BP_Pipe`'s **Event Graph**, add an **Event Tick** node. Drag off and search for **Add Actor Local Offset**. Set the `Delta Location` X to `-600` — this moves the pipe left. But this is frame-rate dependent! If a player runs at 120fps, the pipe moves twice as fast as at 60fps. Fix this by adding a **Get World Delta Seconds** node and multiplying it by `600` using a **Float × Float** node. Connect the result to the `Delta Location` X pin. Delta Seconds gives you the time since the last frame — multiplying speed by it makes movement frame-rate independent, which is essential for fair gameplay across all devices.
steps.tip: Rule: ANY value applied every `Event Tick` that represents speed MUST be multiplied by `Get World Delta Seconds`. No exceptions.

steps.q: A pipe moves left 600 units per tick without Delta Time. On a phone running at 30fps it moves at 18,000 units/sec. On a PC at 120fps it moves at 72,000 units/sec. What is the fix?
opts: Cap the frame rate to 60fps in Project Settings
Multiply the 600 movement value by `Get World Delta Seconds` so speed is consistent regardless of frame rate
Use `Set Actor Location` instead of `Add Actor Local Offset`
Reduce the movement value to 5 so it works at all frame rates
steps.explain: Delta Time is the time elapsed since the last frame. At 30fps it's ~0.033 seconds; at 120fps it's ~0.008 seconds. Multiplying your speed by Delta Time means the total distance per second is always the same: 600 × 0.033 × 30fps = 600 units/sec, and 600 × 0.008 × 120fps = 600 units/sec. This is called frame-rate independent movement and is non-negotiable for fair multiplayer or cross-device games.

steps.title: Auto-Destroying Off-Screen Pipes
steps.body: Pipes that scroll off the left side of the screen still consume RAM and CPU tick cycles. We need to destroy them. In `BP_Pipe`'s **Event Tick**, after moving the pipe, get the pipe's **Get Actor Location** and **Break Vector** to access its `X` component. Feed the `X` value into a **Less Than (<)** node and compare it to `-2000` (well off-screen left). If `true`, connect to a **Destroy Actor** node targeting `Self`. This immediately removes the pipe from the world, freeing its memory. Without this, after 5 minutes of gameplay you could have hundreds of off-screen pipes eating RAM and frame budget — classic memory leak.
steps.tip: Add a `Print String` with the pipe count (using `Get All Actors of Class`) during development to verify pipes are being cleaned up correctly.

steps.q: Why is it critical to destroy `BP_Pipe` actors after they scroll off-screen, rather than just leaving them?
opts: Off-screen actors cause rendering glitches
Every Actor in memory consumes RAM and runs its Event Tick every frame — accumulating hundreds of off-screen pipes will progressively degrade performance and eventually crash the game
UE5 automatically pauses actors that are off-screen
Off-screen actors interfere with the spawner's timer
steps.explain: Every live Actor in UE5 runs its Tick function and occupies memory. In an endless runner, pipes spawn every 2 seconds — after 10 minutes that's 300 un-destroyed pipes all running Tick. This rapidly degrades frame rate and will eventually exhaust RAM. `Destroy Actor` severs all references and returns the memory to the system, keeping the game running indefinitely.

steps.title: Setting Initial Life Span as Backup
steps.body: As a safety net alongside the position-based destruction, select `BP_Pipe` in the **Content Browser** and open its **Class Defaults** (the toolbar button in Blueprint Editor). In **Details**, find **Actor > Initial Life Span**. Set it to `15.0` seconds. This means even if the position check somehow fails — for example if the pipe gets stuck or the player pauses — the pipe will destroy itself after 15 seconds regardless. This dual-destruction approach (position check + lifespan) is defensive programming. At a scrolling speed of 600 units/sec, a pipe takes about 8 seconds to cross the screen, so 15 seconds is a safe upper limit that never triggers during normal play.
steps.tip: Always use defensive programming — two independent safeguards are better than relying on one mechanism that might have edge cases.

steps.q: A pipe scrolls at 600 units/sec and must travel 4000 units off screen to be destroyed. The Initial Life Span is set to 15 seconds. Will the lifespan timer destroy the pipe before the position check during normal gameplay?
opts: Yes — 15 seconds is too short for normal gameplay
No — at 600 units/sec the pipe travels 4000 units in about 6.7 seconds, well before the 15-second lifespan fires, so the position check destroys it first under normal conditions
Both triggers fire simultaneously
The lifespan timer overrides the position check
steps.explain: 4000 units ÷ 600 units/sec = 6.67 seconds to reach the destruction X threshold. Since 6.67 < 15, the position check always fires first during normal play. The 15-second lifespan only activates in edge cases (pausing, bug, extreme lag), acting as a guaranteed cleanup fallback — exactly the role of a safety net.

steps.title: Adjustable Gap Variable
steps.body: Instead of hardcoding the pipe gap at 800 units, make it a variable so you can tweak difficulty. In `BP_Pipe`, go to the **My Blueprint** panel and click `+Variable`. Name it `GapSize`, type `Float`, default value `800`. In the **Construction Script** (not Event Graph — Construction Script runs when you place the actor), get `GapSize`, divide by `2`, and use that result to set `TopPipe`'s `Relative Location Z` (positive half-gap) and `BottomPipe`'s `Relative Location Z` (negative half-gap). Now, when `BP_PipeSpawner` spawns a pipe, it can pass a different `GapSize` value each time to progressively shrink the gap as difficulty increases. This is data-driven design — gameplay parameters live in variables, not buried in hardcoded numbers.
steps.tip: In the **Details** panel for the `GapSize` variable, check **Instance Editable** so you can adjust it per-pipe directly in the viewport.

steps.q: Why is the gap calculation placed in the `Construction Script` rather than `Event BeginPlay`?
opts: Construction Script runs faster than BeginPlay
Construction Script executes in the editor when you place or modify the actor, allowing you to see the gap update live in the viewport as you change GapSize — BeginPlay only runs when the game starts
BeginPlay cannot access Static Mesh components
Construction Script has access to more Blueprint nodes than BeginPlay
steps.explain: The Construction Script runs both in-editor (whenever you modify properties) and at runtime before BeginPlay. This means changing `GapSize` on a placed pipe instantly updates its visual layout in the viewport, giving you real-time feedback during level design. Using BeginPlay would mean you can only see the result after pressing Play, making iteration much slower. Construction Scripts are ideal for any setup that should be visible during editing.

steps.title: Compiling and Testing BP_Pipe
steps.body: Compile and Save `BP_Pipe`. Drag one instance from the **Content Browser** into your level. You should immediately see two tall pipes with a gap between them — the Construction Script positions them based on `GapSize`. Press **Play**. The pipe should scroll left at a consistent speed. After it passes X=-2000 it should disappear from the Outliner, confirming the destruction logic works. If it doesn't move, check that `Add Actor Local Offset` is connected to the execution wire from `Event Tick` and that `Delta Seconds` is being multiplied. If it doesn't disappear, check the Less Than comparison value and ensure `Destroy Actor` is actually connected.
steps.tip: Use **Window > Statistics** to monitor actor count in real-time — watch it stay flat as pipes spawn and destroy, confirming no memory leak.

steps.q: During testing, the pipe scrolls but never disappears even after going far off-screen. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The pipe's `Initial Life Span` is set too high
The `Less Than (<)` node's comparison value is too negative (e.g., -99999), or the `Destroy Actor` node is not connected to the `True` execution pin of the `Branch` node
The `Add Actor Local Offset` value is too small
The `Construction Script` is interfering with the destruction logic
steps.explain: The most common causes of a pipe not destroying are: the threshold value is wrong (so the pipe's X never reaches it), or the `Destroy Actor` is not actually connected in the execution chain. Always check that the `Branch` node's `True` pin leads to `Destroy Actor`, and that the comparison threshold is a reasonable value like -2000 rather than an extreme number.

steps.title: Updating BP_PipeSpawner
steps.body: Now that `BP_Pipe` exists, open `BP_PipeSpawner`. Find the `SpawnPipeEvent` Custom Event. The **Spawn Actor from Class** node's `Class` pin was left empty — click it and select `BP_Pipe`. For the **Spawn Transform**, we need a proper location. Use **Get Actor Location** on `Self`, then **Break Vector**. Take the `X` and `Y` values, but replace `Z` with a **Random Float in Range** node (Min=`200`, Max=`700`). Reconstruct the vector with **Make Vector** and connect it to the `Spawn Transform` `Location` pin. This spawns each pipe at the spawner's X/Y position but at a random height, creating the varied challenge. Ensure the spawner is placed far to the right (X=3000) in your level.
steps.tip: You can expose the `Min` and `Max` values of the Random Float as variables on the spawner to tune difficulty from the editor without opening Blueprint.

steps.q: After setting up the Spawner, pipes spawn but always at the same height. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The timer interval is too short
The `Random Float in Range` node's Min and Max are set to the same value, or the node is not connected to the Z pin of `Make Vector`
The pipe's `Construction Script` is overriding the spawn location
The `Spawn Actor from Class` node doesn't support transform overrides
steps.explain: If Min equals Max, `Random Float in Range` always returns that one value — no randomness. Also check that the output of `Random Float in Range` is actually wired to the `Z` pin of `Make Vector`, not left floating. Both mistakes produce pipes at identical heights every time.

steps.title: Progressive Difficulty: Shrinking Gap
steps.body: To make the game get harder over time, we'll shrink the pipe gap every 10 pipes. In `BP_PipeSpawner`, add an `Integer` variable named `PipesSpawned`, default `0`. In `SpawnPipeEvent`, after spawning the pipe, increment `PipesSpawned` by `1` using **Integer + Integer**. Add a **Branch** node checking if `PipesSpawned % 10 == 0` (use the **Modulo** node then an **Equal** node comparing to `0`). If `true`, add a `Float` variable named `CurrentGapSize` (default `800`). Subtract `50` from it and **Clamp** it between `400` and `800`. On the spawned pipe reference returned by **Spawn Actor from Class**, call `SetGapSize` (you'll need to make this a function in `BP_Pipe` that sets the `GapSize` variable and re-runs the Construction Script logic). This makes the game progressively harder every 10 pipes.
steps.tip: Use `Print String` to display `CurrentGapSize` during testing so you can see difficulty ramping up in real-time.

steps.q: Why is `Modulo (%)` used to check if the gap should shrink, rather than checking `PipesSpawned == 10`, `== 20`, `== 30` etc.?
opts: Modulo is faster to compute than multiple equality checks
Modulo gives the remainder of division — `PipesSpawned % 10 == 0` is true at 10, 20, 30, 40... automatically, covering all future milestones with a single check that scales infinitely
Equality checks don't work with Integer variables in Blueprint
Modulo is the only way to compare integers in Blueprint
steps.explain: Manually checking `== 10`, `== 20` etc. would require adding a new branch for every milestone and breaks if you want different intervals. `Modulo` elegantly handles all cases: any number divisible by 10 gives remainder 0, so `% 10 == 0` fires at pipes 10, 20, 30, 40... forever — one node that scales to infinity.

steps.title: Increasing Scroll Speed Over Time
steps.body: Alongside shrinking gaps, we can increase pipe speed over time. Add a `Float` variable to `BP_PipeSpawner` named `PipeSpeed`, default `600`. In `SpawnPipeEvent`, after the gap shrink logic, also increase `PipeSpeed` by `10` every 10 pipes (same modulo check). Clamp it between `600` and `1200`. When spawning, get the pipe reference from **Spawn Actor from Class** and call a custom function `SetScrollSpeed` on `BP_Pipe` that stores the value in a variable. In `BP_Pipe`'s Event Tick, replace the hardcoded `600` with this `ScrollSpeed` variable. Now both gap and speed tighten as the player improves. Two compounding difficulty levers create a much deeper challenge curve than either alone.
steps.tip: Increasing speed AND shrinking gaps simultaneously is powerful — consider adding a slight delay between the two escalations so players can adapt.

steps.q: Why is clamping `PipeSpeed` between 600 and 1200 important?
opts: Blueprint Float variables can only store values up to 1200

Without clamping, speed increases infinitely — eventually pipes move so fast they teleport past the bird's collision sphere each frame without triggering a hit, making the game impossible to lose after a certain point
Clamping improves Blueprint compile speed
Values above 1200 cause the `Add Actor Local Offset` node to crash
steps.explain: This is a real physics bug called 'tunneling' — when an object moves so fast in a single frame that it passes completely through another object without overlap registering. At extreme speeds, pipes would fly through the bird without killing it. Clamping at 1200 keeps movement within safe collision detection limits while still providing significant difficulty increase.

steps.title: Compile, Test, and Tune
steps.body: Compile and Save both `BP_Pipe` and `BP_PipeSpawner`. Delete any manually placed test pipes from your level. Ensure one `BP_PipeSpawner` is placed at `X=3000, Y=0, Z=300`. Press **Play**. Pipes should spawn every 2 seconds at varying heights, scroll left at 600 units/sec, and disappear when they leave the screen. After 10 pipes, the gap should visibly shrink. Watch the Outliner — the pipe count should stay stable (never growing). If pipes don't vary in height, check the `Random Float in Range` connection. If speed doesn't increase, add a `Print String` showing `PipeSpeed` after each modulo check to debug the increment logic.
steps.tip: Set the spawn interval to `0.1` seconds temporarily to quickly generate 20+ pipes and verify the difficulty curve without waiting.

steps.q: During testing, the pipe count in the Outliner keeps growing every 2 seconds and never goes down. What does this indicate?
opts: The timer is firing too quickly
The destruction logic is not working — pipes are spawning but never being destroyed, confirming a memory leak
The Construction Script is spawning extra pipes
The `BP_PipeSpawner` is placed too close to the camera
steps.explain: A rising actor count that never decreases is the classic signature of a memory leak. Either the `Destroy Actor` node is not connected, the threshold X value is never reached, or the `Initial Life Span` is set to 0 (which means live forever in UE5, not instant destruction). Fix the destruction logic immediately before adding more systems — leaks compound and become impossible to isolate later.

steps.title: OnHit Event on the Bird
steps.body: Open `BP_Bird`. Select the `CollisionSphere` component. In the **Details** panel scroll down to **Events** and click the `+` next to **On Component Hit**. This adds an `OnComponentHit` event node to the Event Graph. This event fires when the sphere physically collides with any `BlockAllDynamic` object — including our pipe meshes. From this event, drag off and call a Custom Event named `HandleDeath`. Separating the hit detection from the death logic keeps your graph clean and allows you to call `HandleDeath` from multiple sources (hitting a pipe, hitting the floor) without duplicating code. This pattern — a single death handler called from multiple triggers — is fundamental game architecture.
steps.tip: Always separate 'what caused the death' from 'what happens when you die' — this makes adding new kill conditions trivial later.

steps.q: Why create a separate `HandleDeath` Custom Event rather than putting all death logic directly inside `OnComponentHit`?
opts: Custom Events run faster than inline logic

Separating the logic means multiple triggers (pipe hit, floor hit, time limit) can all call one central `HandleDeath` function without duplicating code — changing death behavior only requires editing one place
OnComponentHit cannot contain more than 3 connected nodes
Custom Events are required for any logic that involves destroying actors
steps.explain: This is the DRY principle — Don't Repeat Yourself. If death logic lived inside `OnComponentHit`, you'd need to copy it into every new kill condition. With a central `HandleDeath` Custom Event, adding a new way to die (lava, timer, falling) just means adding one more call to `HandleDeath`. You only maintain the actual death behavior in one location.

steps.title: Implementing HandleDeath
steps.body: Inside the `HandleDeath` Custom Event, we need to: stop the bird from moving, stop pipe spawning, and show the game over screen. First, add a **Boolean** variable to `BP_Bird` named `bIsDead`, default `false`. At the very start of `HandleDeath`, add a **Branch** checking `bIsDead` — if `true`, return immediately (do nothing). If `false`, set `bIsDead` to `true`. This prevents `HandleDeath` from firing twice (two simultaneous collisions can both trigger the event in the same frame). Then set the `BirdMovement` component's **Active** to `false` using **Set Active** to freeze movement. Finally, use **Get All Actors of Class** (`BP_PipeSpawner`) and call **Destroy Actor** on the result to stop new pipes from spawning.
steps.tip: The `bIsDead` guard at the start of any death/completion handler is essential — simultaneous events are extremely common in physics-based games.

steps.q: Why is a `bIsDead` Boolean check placed at the start of `HandleDeath` before any other logic?
opts: To improve Blueprint compile performance

Physics collisions can trigger the same event multiple times in a single frame — the Boolean guard ensures death logic only runs once, preventing duplicate effects like showing the game over screen twice
Boolean variables are required before any Branch node in Blueprint
It prevents the bird from dying during the first 2 seconds of gameplay
steps.explain: When the bird hits a pipe at speed, the physics engine can register multiple overlapping collision contacts in a single frame — all firing `OnComponentHit` simultaneously. Without the guard, `HandleDeath` could execute 3-4 times in one frame, spawning multiple game over screens, decrementing score multiple times, or causing undefined behavior. The Boolean guard is a simple one-time lock that prevents this.

steps.title: Floor Death Detection
steps.body: The bird should also die when it hits the floor. In your level, select the floor mesh you created in Module 10. In its **Details** panel, under **Collision**, set **Collision Presets** to `BlockAllDynamic`. This means the bird's `CollisionSphere` (which is also `BlockAllDynamic`) will generate a Hit event when they meet. Since the bird already has `OnComponentHit` calling `HandleDeath`, the floor collision is handled automatically — no extra Blueprint needed. This is the power of a unified collision response system. However, you should also add a Z-position check in `BP_Bird`'s `Event Tick`: if `Actor Location Z < 50`, also call `HandleDeath`. This is a failsafe in case collision detection misses the hit.
steps.tip: Always add both physics-based AND position-based death detection — physics can miss fast-moving objects; position checks never will.

steps.q: The bird dies when hitting a pipe but not when hitting the floor. The floor has `BlockAllDynamic` collision. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The floor mesh doesn't support collision
The bird's `CollisionSphere` collision preset may be set to `OverlapAllDynamic` instead of `BlockAllDynamic` — both objects need to be set to Block each other for a Hit event to fire
The `OnComponentHit` event is specific to pipes only
Floor collision requires a separate `OnComponentHit` event
steps.explain: In UE5, a Hit event only fires when BOTH colliding objects have collision responses set to Block each other. If the bird's `CollisionSphere` is set to Overlap (not Block) against WorldStatic objects (which floors typically are), no Hit event fires. Check the bird's collision profile — it needs to Block WorldStatic or you need to change the floor to Dynamic.

steps.title: Death Visual Feedback
steps.body: A silent death is unsatisfying. In `HandleDeath`, after stopping movement, add visual and audio feedback. Use **Spawn Emitter at Location** (search for it in Blueprint) with the `P_Explosion` particle from Starter Content to play a burst effect at the bird's location. Use **Play Sound at Location** with any short `Starter_Sound` asset. Then add a **Delay** of `1.0` second before showing the Game Over UI (which we'll build in the next module). The delay gives the particle effect time to play before the UI appears. This sequence — physics death → visual feedback → delay → UI — is the standard game over flow used in virtually every arcade game.
steps.tip: Use `Spawn Emitter at Location` for one-shot particle effects at a point in space — it auto-destroys when finished, no cleanup needed.

steps.q: Why add a `Delay` node between the death particle effect and showing the Game Over UI?
opts: The Delay prevents the UI from rendering before the particle is loaded

Showing UI immediately at the moment of death feels jarring — a brief delay lets the particle effect play and gives the player a moment to process what happened before the screen changes, improving game feel
The Delay is required for the Game Over widget to initialize correctly
Without a Delay, the UI would appear behind the particle effect
steps.explain: Instant UI transitions feel cheap and abrupt. The 1-second delay serves three purposes: it lets the death particle finish playing, it gives the player's brain time to register the death, and it makes the game feel more polished. This micro-delay is a small but significant piece of 'game feel' — the intangible quality that makes a game feel satisfying to play.

steps.title: Game State and Score Variable
steps.body: Score should not live in `BP_Bird` — if the bird is destroyed on death, the score disappears with it. Score belongs in the **Game State**. In the **Content Browser**, create a new Blueprint Class with `GameStateBase` as parent. Name it `BP_FlappyGameState`. Add an `Integer` variable named `Score`, default `0`. Also add a `Integer` variable named `HighScore`, default `0`. Open `BP_FlappyBirdGameMode` and set **Game State Class** to `BP_FlappyGameState`. The Game State persists for the lifetime of the game session, surviving individual actor deaths. This is the correct architectural home for persistent game data like score, lives, and high scores.
steps.tip: Game State = data that belongs to the match/session. Player State = data that belongs to one player. Game Mode = rules. Never mix these up.

steps.q: Why should the `Score` variable live in `BP_FlappyGameState` rather than in `BP_Bird`?
opts: Game State Blueprints have access to more math nodes

When the bird is destroyed on death, any variables stored inside it are also destroyed. Game State persists for the entire session, so the score survives the bird's death and can be displayed on the Game Over screen
Integer variables cannot be stored in Pawn Blueprints
Game State automatically syncs score to the leaderboard
steps.explain: Blueprint variables live and die with their owner Actor. When `Destroy Actor` is called on `BP_Bird`, everything inside it — including a Score variable — is garbage collected. `BP_FlappyGameState` is never destroyed during normal gameplay, making it the reliable persistent container for session data like score. This is a core architectural principle: match-level data belongs in Game State.

steps.title: Score Trigger: OnComponentBeginOverlap
steps.body: Open `BP_Pipe`. Select the `ScoreTrigger` box collision component. In **Details > Events**, click `+` next to **On Component Begin Overlap**. This event fires when any actor enters the box. In the event, check that the `Other Actor` is a `BP_Bird` using **Cast to BP_Bird** — only award a point if the bird passes through (not some other actor). If the cast succeeds, use **Get Game State** then **Cast to BP_FlappyGameState**. Drag off and increment the `Score` variable using **Integer + Integer** (add 1). Set the result back to `Score`. Also check if the new score exceeds `HighScore` and update it if so. This is the complete scoring pipeline.
steps.tip: Always verify `Other Actor` identity with a Cast before taking action — collision events fire for ANY overlapping actor, not just the player.

steps.q: Why cast `Other Actor` to `BP_Bird` before incrementing the score?
opts: Casting improves the performance of the overlap event

The `On Component Begin Overlap` event fires for any actor that enters the trigger, including spawned particles or debris — casting ensures you only increment score when specifically the player bird passes through, not any random object
Blueprint requires a cast before accessing any variable
The cast prevents the score from going negative
steps.explain: Without the cast, any actor that overlaps the score trigger — a debug sphere, a test actor, even certain particles — would incorrectly increment the score. The cast to `BP_Bird` is a filter that ensures only the player character's passage through the gap is counted as a point. Always filter overlap events by actor type.

steps.title: Displaying Score in HUD
steps.body: Create a new **Widget Blueprint** (right-click Content Browser → User Interface → Widget Blueprint). Name it `WBP_HUD`. Open it. In the **Designer** tab, drag a **Text** widget from the Palette onto the canvas. Position it at the top center. Name it `ScoreText`. Switch to the **Graph** tab. Add an **Event Tick** (or bind to a delegate — but Tick is simpler for now). Get **Game State**, cast to `BP_FlappyGameState`, get `Score`, convert to String with **Integer to String**, and set it to `ScoreText` using **Set Text**. To show this HUD, open `BP_Bird`'s `BeginPlay`, add **Create Widget** (class: `WBP_HUD`), then **Add to Viewport**. The HUD will now appear and update every frame.
steps.tip: For production games, use Event Dispatchers instead of Tick to update UI — but Tick is fine for learning and small projects.

steps.q: What is the correct sequence to make a Widget Blueprint appear on screen during gameplay?
opts: Create Widget → Set Visibility → Add to Viewport
Create Widget → Add to Viewport — `Add to Viewport` both finalizes creation and makes it visible on screen
Add Widget to HUD → Create Widget
Create Widget → Add to Player Screen → Compile
steps.explain: `Create Widget` instantiates the widget in memory but does NOT display it. `Add to Viewport` then places it on screen. This two-step process is intentional — it lets you configure the widget between creation and display. Skipping `Add to Viewport` is the most common reason widgets 'don't appear' for beginners.

steps.title: High Score with Save Game
steps.body: High scores should survive app restarts. UE5 has a built-in **Save Game** system. Create a Blueprint Class with `SaveGame` as parent, named `BP_FlappySaveGame`. Add an `Integer` variable `SavedHighScore`. In `BP_FlappyGameState`, on `BeginPlay`, use **Load Game from Slot** (slot name: `FlappyData`, user index: `0`). Cast the result to `BP_FlappySaveGame` and read `SavedHighScore` into the `HighScore` variable. When a new high score is set, create a new `BP_FlappySaveGame` instance, set its `SavedHighScore`, and call **Save Game to Slot** with the same slot name. This persists to the device's storage and survives app restarts — the same system used by AAA games for save data.
steps.tip: Always check if `Load Game from Slot` returns `null` (no save file exists yet) and handle that case by starting with a default high score of 0.

steps.q: What happens if `Load Game from Slot` returns `null` and you immediately try to cast it and read `SavedHighScore`?
opts: It returns 0 automatically as the default Integer value

The cast fails and any Blueprint logic downstream of the cast output will not execute — if you don't handle the null case, your high score display will show nothing or an error on the first ever launch
UE5 crashes when casting null
The Save Game system creates a new file automatically
steps.explain: When no save file exists (first launch), `Load Game from Slot` returns null. Casting null produces a failed cast — the Cast node's failed output pin fires. If you don't handle this (e.g., by branching on the `Is Valid` pin), any code expecting a valid `BP_FlappySaveGame` reference will simply not run, leaving `HighScore` at 0 from Blueprint initialization. This is correct behavior — just make sure to explicitly handle the null case to avoid silent failures.

title: Module 12: Game UI — Menus & Game Over
desc: Build the complete UI flow — main menu, in-game HUD with live score, and a game over screen with high score and restart.

steps.title: Creating WBP_MainMenu
steps.body: Create a new **Widget Blueprint** named `WBP_MainMenu`. Open it in the **Designer** tab. Set the canvas size to `1080x1920` (portrait mobile). Add a **Vertical Box** widget, anchor it to fill the full canvas (stretch both ways in the Anchors dropdown). Inside the Vertical Box, add a **Text** widget for the game title — set it to `FLAPPY BIRD`, font size `72`, bold. Add a **Spacer** below it (size Y=100). Add a **Button** widget. Inside the button, add a **Text** widget reading `PLAY`. Add another **Spacer** and a **Button** reading `QUIT`. The Vertical Box automatically stacks them vertically and the anchoring ensures it scales correctly on all screen sizes.
steps.tip: Always anchor UI elements relative to the canvas edges, not at fixed pixel positions — fixed positions break on different screen sizes.

steps.q: Why is a `Vertical Box` used to arrange menu buttons rather than placing each widget at a fixed X/Y position on the canvas?
opts: Vertical Box widgets render faster than individually placed widgets
A Vertical Box automatically stacks and spaces its children, making layout maintenance easy — adding a new button just requires adding it to the box, not manually repositioning every element below it
Fixed position widgets don't support click events
Vertical Box is required for widgets to be visible on mobile screens
steps.explain: Manual positioning becomes a maintenance nightmare — adding one new element means repositioning everything below it. Layout containers like `Vertical Box` and `Horizontal Box` automatically arrange their children, handling spacing and order. This is why virtually every UI framework from CSS Flexbox to UE5 UMG uses container-based layouts rather than manual coordinate placement.

steps.title: Wiring the Play Button
steps.body: In `WBP_MainMenu`'s **Graph** tab, select the PLAY button in the Designer and click `+` next to **On Clicked** in the **Details** panel. This creates an `OnClicked` event in the graph. Inside it, use **Open Level (by Name)** and type `L_FlappyBird` (your game level name exactly as you saved it). Also call **Remove from Parent** on `Self` to close the menu widget before opening the level. For the QUIT button, add another `OnClicked` event and use **Quit Game**. The `Open Level` node handles all scene loading and cleanup automatically — you don't need to manually destroy actors from the current level.
steps.tip: Use `Open Level by Name` not `Open Level by Object Reference` for runtime level loading — object references bake the level into memory at startup.

steps.q: Why call `Remove from Parent` on the menu widget before `Open Level`?
opts: Open Level crashes if a widget is still on screen
While Open Level would destroy the widget anyway, explicitly removing it first ensures a clean transition and prevents a single frame where the menu widget appears on top of the loading new level
Remove from Parent is required to free the button's memory
Open Level does not work while any widget is displayed
steps.explain: When `Open Level` executes, it destroys the current world and all its actors including widgets — but there can be a one-frame flash where the old UI is visible during the transition. Explicitly calling `Remove from Parent` first ensures the menu disappears cleanly before the level swap begins, producing a cleaner transition. It's a polish detail but highly noticeable without it.

steps.title: Creating a Menu Level
steps.body: The main menu should have its own level — not the gameplay level. Go to **File > New Level** and select **Empty Level**. Save it as `L_MainMenu`. This level has no gameplay — it just shows the `WBP_MainMenu` widget. Create a new Blueprint Class with `Actor` as parent, named `BP_MenuManager`. On `Event BeginPlay`, create and show `WBP_MainMenu` using **Create Widget** → **Add to Viewport**. Also set input mode to UI only with **Set Input Mode UI Only** and **Show Mouse Cursor**. Place `BP_MenuManager` in `L_MainMenu`. In **Project Settings > Maps & Modes**, set `L_MainMenu` as the **Default Map** so the game always starts on the menu.
steps.tip: Set `L_MainMenu` as Default Map in Project Settings so builds always start at the menu — never at the game level directly.

steps.q: Why create a separate `L_MainMenu` level rather than showing the menu widget in the gameplay level?
opts: Widget Blueprints cannot be shown during gameplay

A dedicated menu level keeps the game and menu cleanly separated — the game level doesn't load until the player chooses to play, saving memory and ensuring the first thing a player sees is the menu, not the game mid-play
Menu levels load faster than gameplay levels
Two widgets cannot be shown in the same level simultaneously
steps.explain: Mixing menu and gameplay in one level means gameplay systems are loaded and ticking while the player is on the menu, wasting resources. Separate levels enforce clean boundaries: menu level = UI only, gameplay level = game systems. It's also safer — you can't accidentally start playing before pressing Play, and level transitions give a natural loading moment.

steps.title: Setting Input Mode Correctly
steps.body: When the main menu appears, mouse clicks must reach the UI widgets. In `BP_MenuManager`, after creating and showing the menu widget, call **Set Input Mode UI Only** (Target: Get Player Controller). This directs all input to the UMG widget layer, enabling mouse cursor visibility and button clicks. When the game starts (after pressing PLAY), the game level must switch back to game input. In `BP_Bird`'s `BeginPlay`, call **Set Input Mode Game Only** and **Set Show Mouse Cursor (false)**. Forgetting to switch input modes is one of the most common UMG bugs — clicking Play and finding that the game ignores all keyboard input because the UI mode is still active.
steps.tip: Always pair every `Set Input Mode UI Only` with a corresponding `Set Input Mode Game Only` when transitioning to gameplay.

steps.q: After pressing PLAY on the main menu and loading the game level, Space Bar doesn't make the bird jump. What is the most likely cause?
opts: The jump input binding was lost during level loading
Input mode was set to `UI Only` on the menu and never switched back to `Game Only` — keyboard and gamepad events are being consumed by the (now invisible) UI layer instead of reaching the player pawn
The Bird Blueprint needs to be recompiled after level loading
Space Bar input requires a separate input mapping context for each level
steps.explain: Input mode persists across level loads within a session. If the menu set `UI Only` and the game level doesn't set `Game Only`, all keyboard input continues to route to the UI system even though no visible UI is active. The Space Bar press is swallowed by UMG silently. Always reset input mode at the start of gameplay.

steps.title: Creating WBP_GameOver
steps.body: Create a **Widget Blueprint** named `WBP_GameOver`. In the Designer, add a full-screen semi-transparent dark panel (Image widget, color `#000000` at 60% opacity) to dim the gameplay behind the UI. On top, add a **Vertical Box** with: a **Text** reading `GAME OVER` (font 80, white), a **Text** reading `Score: 0` named `ScoreDisplay`, a **Text** reading `Best: 0` named `HighScoreDisplay`, a **Button** with `PLAY AGAIN` text, and a **Button** with `MENU` text. The dimmed background is a crucial UX pattern — it tells the player the game has paused without obscuring the final game state they see when they died.
steps.tip: Use a semi-transparent overlay (not fully opaque) on game over screens so players can briefly see where they died — it adds context to the death.

steps.q: Why is a semi-transparent dark overlay placed behind the Game Over text rather than a fully opaque panel?
opts: Fully opaque panels cause rendering errors in UMG

The semi-transparent overlay lets players briefly see their final game state while clearly communicating that the game has ended — a fully opaque panel would abruptly hide everything, losing the context of where and how they died
Semi-transparent panels use less memory
UMG buttons don't work on top of fully opaque backgrounds
steps.explain: In game design, context matters at death — seeing your bird fall into a pipe is part of the feedback loop. A semi-transparent overlay preserves this visual context while clearly communicating the game over state. Fully opaque panels feel like a crash or a harsh cut, breaking immersion. This is why virtually every game uses a dimmed-but-visible background for pause/death overlays.

steps.title: Populating Score on Show
steps.body: In `WBP_GameOver`'s Graph, add a custom function named `SetScores` that takes two Integer inputs: `FinalScore` and `FinalHighScore`. Inside, convert both to Strings and set `ScoreDisplay` and `HighScoreDisplay` text accordingly. In `BP_Bird`'s `HandleDeath`, after the `Delay`, use **Create Widget** (`WBP_GameOver`), then immediately call `SetScores` on the widget reference, passing the current `Score` and `HighScore` from the Game State. Then call **Add to Viewport**. Calling `SetScores` before `Add to Viewport` ensures the correct scores are shown the instant the widget appears — no flash of zero values.
steps.tip: Always populate widget data BEFORE calling `Add to Viewport` to avoid a single-frame flash of empty/default values.

steps.q: What visual bug occurs if you call `Add to Viewport` before calling `SetScores` on the Game Over widget?
opts: The widget appears in the wrong screen position
For one frame (or more on slow devices), the widget shows its default values (Score: 0, Best: 0) before the correct values are set — a visible flash that looks like a bug
The widget's buttons stop responding to input
The score text appears behind the background overlay
steps.explain: Widgets render the frame they're added to viewport. If data isn't set first, the initial render uses default variable values (0). On fast devices this might be invisible, but on slow devices this zero-flash is clearly visible and looks broken. Setting data first guarantees the very first render has correct values — no flicker.

steps.title: Play Again and Menu Buttons
steps.body: In `WBP_GameOver`, add `OnClicked` events for both buttons. For PLAY AGAIN: call **Open Level by Name** with `L_FlappyBird`. This reloads the entire game level from scratch — all actors re-initialize, score resets to 0, pipes restart. For MENU: call **Open Level by Name** with `L_MainMenu`. Also set **Input Mode UI Only** before showing this widget (call it from `HandleDeath` in `BP_Bird` before creating the Game Over widget). The simplest restart mechanism in UE5 is a full level reload — it's clean, requires no manual reset logic, and guarantees a fresh state. More advanced games use soft resets, but level reload is perfectly appropriate for arcade games.
steps.tip: Full level reload is the cleanest restart — zero state management overhead. Only optimize to soft reset when load times become a user experience problem.

steps.q: What is the main advantage of restarting the game by reloading the level (`Open Level`) rather than manually resetting all variables?
opts: Open Level is the only Blueprint node that can restart a game

Level reload guarantees every Actor, variable, and system is in its exact initial state with zero risk of leftover state from the previous run — manual resets are error-prone and will always miss something eventually
Level reload is faster than manual resets
Manual resets don't work while a Widget is on screen
steps.explain: Manual resets require you to track every single piece of state and reset it explicitly — miss one variable and you have a subtle bug (e.g., score not resetting, dead bird still being dead). Level reload is a nuclear option that guarantees pristine state by letting UE5 rebuild everything from scratch. For a small arcade game with no persistent world state, this is the correct approach. The tiny extra load time is an acceptable tradeoff for zero state bugs.

steps.title: Score Animation: Pop Effect
steps.body: To make the score feel exciting, add a pop animation when the score increments. In `WBP_HUD`, select `ScoreText`. In the **Animations** panel (bottom left of UMG Designer), click `+Animation` and name it `ScorePop`. Add a keyframe at time `0.0`: Scale `1.0, 1.0`. At time `0.1`: Scale `1.4, 1.4`. At time `0.2`: Scale `1.0, 1.0`. This creates a quick scale-up-and-back bounce. In `BP_FlappyGameState`, add an **Event Dispatcher** named `OnScoreChanged`. Broadcast it every time `Score` increments. In `WBP_HUD`'s BeginPlay, bind to `OnScoreChanged` and play the `ScorePop` animation using **Play Animation**. Event Dispatchers decouple the score logic from the UI — the Game State doesn't need to know anything about widgets.
steps.tip: Event Dispatchers are the correct way for game logic to notify UI — never have game systems directly reference or manipulate widget variables.

steps.q: Why use an Event Dispatcher in Game State to notify the HUD of score changes, rather than having Game State directly call a function on the HUD widget?
opts: Event Dispatchers are faster than direct function calls

Direct references from Game State to HUD create tight coupling — Game State would need to know the HUD exists and hold a reference to it. Event Dispatchers are loosely coupled: Game State just broadcasts an event, and any listener (including multiple HUDs) responds independently
Game State Blueprints cannot hold references to Widget Blueprints
Direct function calls don't work across different Blueprint classes
steps.explain: If Game State directly calls functions on the HUD, it must hold a reference to the HUD — creating a dependency. If you ever remove the HUD or add a second one, you must update Game State. Event Dispatchers invert this: Game State just shouts 'score changed!' and doesn't care who's listening. The HUD subscribes independently. This loose coupling is fundamental to maintainable game architecture.

steps.title: Adding Background Music
steps.body: Import an audio file (MP3 or WAV) by dragging it into the Content Browser — UE5 converts it to a `.uasset` sound wave automatically. Right-click the Sound Wave and select **Create Cue** to generate a `Sound Cue` asset. Open the Sound Cue and add a **Looping** modifier node between the Wave Player and the Output. In `BP_MenuManager`'s BeginPlay, use **Play Sound 2D** with the looping Sound Cue and store the result in a variable `MusicComponent`. When `L_FlappyBird` loads, play a different (faster-paced) music cue. `Play Sound 2D` is used for UI and music because it has no 3D spatial position — the volume doesn't change based on where the camera is, which is correct for background music.
steps.tip: Use royalty-free music from sites like OpenGameArt.org or FreeMusicArchive.org for your games to avoid copyright issues.

steps.q: Why use `Play Sound 2D` for background music instead of `Play Sound at Location`?
opts: Play Sound 2D is louder than Play Sound at Location

Background music has no 3D position — it should play at consistent volume regardless of where the camera or player is. `Play Sound at Location` attenuates with distance, making music fade when the camera moves away from its source point
Play Sound at Location doesn't support looping
Play Sound 2D loads faster from disk
steps.explain: `Play Sound at Location` uses 3D spatial audio — volume decreases as the listener moves away from the source point. If music played from a fixed world location (e.g., 0,0,0), it would fade as the camera moves, which is completely wrong for background music. `Play Sound 2D` bypasses spatial calculation entirely, playing at constant volume everywhere, which is exactly right for music and UI sounds.

steps.title: Screen Shake on Death
steps.body: A screen shake on death dramatically improves impact. In UE5, create a Blueprint Class with `CameraShakeBase` as parent, named `BS_DeathShake`. Open it. In the **Details** panel, under **Oscillation**, set **Duration** to `0.4`, **Pitch Amplitude** `5.0`, **Yaw Amplitude** `5.0`, **Frequency** `25`. In `BP_Bird`'s `HandleDeath`, before the Delay, use **Get Player Camera Manager** then **Start Camera Shake** with `BS_DeathShake` and scale `1.0`. The camera shake communicates physical impact through the screen — it's a fundamental technique in every action game. Even subtle shakes (duration 0.3, amplitude 3) dramatically improve the feeling of consequences.
steps.tip: Less is more with camera shake — values that feel strong in isolation feel perfectly tuned in context. Start subtle and amplify only if it feels weak.

steps.q: What is the primary purpose of a camera shake on death in an arcade game?
opts: It is required for the death animation to play correctly
It provides physical, tactile-feeling feedback through the screen that communicates impact and consequence — making the death feel weighty rather than just a silent state change
Camera shake improves rendering performance during transitions
It prevents the player from seeing the death particle effect too clearly
steps.explain: Camera shake is a form of game feel — the collection of audio, visual, and tactile responses that make game events feel satisfying and impactful. Without it, death is a quiet, weightless state change. With it, the player's screen physically reacts to the impact, creating an instinctive emotional response. This is why every game from mobile casual to AAA uses screen shake for impacts.

steps.title: Mobile Touch Input
steps.body: The game currently uses Space Bar — which doesn't exist on mobile. In `BP_Bird`, add a second input binding. In `Event Graph`, add a **Event Any Key** node (or add Touch input in Project Settings). Actually the better approach: in **Project Settings > Input**, add a new Action Mapping named `IA_Tap` and bind it to `Touch`. In `BP_Bird`'s BeginPlay, add `IMC_Player`'s `IA_Tap` to the Enhanced Input system alongside `IA_Jump`. In the Event Graph, add an `IA_Tap` event node from its **Started** pin — wire it to the same **Do Once → Set Velocity** jump logic. Now tapping anywhere on the screen in mobile jumps the bird, and Space Bar still works on PC. Both inputs coexist without conflict.
steps.tip: Always support both touch AND keyboard in mobile games — testers, designers, and journalists often test on PC even for mobile-only games.

steps.q: Why bind `IA_Tap` (Touch) as a separate action rather than replacing the Space Bar binding with Touch?
opts: Touch and keyboard inputs use different Blueprint event types and cannot share a binding

Keeping both bindings active simultaneously means the game works on both mobile (touch) and PC (keyboard) without needing separate builds or input mode switching — maximum compatibility with zero extra code
Mobile touch events require a separate Input Mapping Context
Space Bar binding is automatically removed when a Touch binding is added
steps.explain: UE5's Enhanced Input system allows any number of bindings for the same Action across different devices. By keeping Space Bar AND adding Touch to the same IMC, the game automatically works on any device — PC players use keyboard, mobile players tap the screen, and both work in parallel. This cross-platform input compatibility is a professional standard and costs nothing to implement.

steps.title: Building the APK
steps.body: Your Flappy Bird clone is complete. To build the final APK: open your project in **Android Studio** via `npx cap open android`. In Android Studio, go to **Build > Generate Signed Bundle / APK**. Select **APK**, click **Next**. If you have no keystore, click **Create new** — fill in a key alias (e.g., `flappy_key`) and password. Android requires a signing key to install APKs on devices. Select `release` build variant and click **Finish**. The APK will be in `android/app/release/`. Transfer it to your device and install with `adb install app-release.apk` or share via Google Drive. This signed APK is suitable for submission to the Google Play Store.
steps.tip: Store your keystore file (.jks) safely — you need the same key to update your app on the Play Store. Losing it means publishing a brand new app.

steps.q: Why is a signing keystore required to build a release APK, and what happens if you lose the keystore file?
opts: The keystore is only needed for Play Store submissions, not for installing APKs

The keystore cryptographically identifies you as the app's author — Android uses it to verify updates come from the original developer. Losing it means you cannot publish updates to an existing Play Store listing; you must create an entirely new app entry
The keystore improves APK load time on Android devices
Keystores are temporary and a new one is generated automatically for each build
steps.explain: Android's signing system is a security mechanism — only the holder of the original keystore can publish updates to an existing app. If you lose it, your app on the Play Store is effectively orphaned: you can't update it, only replace it with a new listing (losing all ratings, reviews, and installs). Always back up your keystore to multiple secure locations. This is one of the most devastating mistakes an indie developer can make.
